title: Lesson 274
transcript: Thank you. All right, one of the most important things that we have to protect from in security management is what we call social engineering. We've just talked about threat modeling and some of the methodologies that exist out there to allow us to gauge how important certain threats are to defend against but one of the most important threats that every organization has is what we call social engineering and social engineering is very important for us to address simply because it attacks the human element. In fact, a good definition of social engineering is any method that uses intimidation or deception to try and get people to provide information that they shouldn't. And by nature people are They tend to be really helpful and provide information. And that's one of the ways that attackers are always getting in through social engineering attacks. And these are some of the examples of the ones that are most prevalent. You've all heard of phishing attacks, for example. Phishing attacks are any method where the attacker pretends to be a legitimate source like your bank or something else. to try and get you to provide information that they shouldn't. And, you know, there's different clever names that people have come up with to talk about the different flavors of phishing attacks like farming and spear phishing and whaling and vishing and so on but they're really just examples of the same thing and that is to pretend to be some authorized source that tries to get information from a unwilling or uninformed victim for example. The best way to protect against social engineering attacks is always through education, awareness and training and to have really good policies in place that dictate what you can and cannot do or what you should. and should not do. Now pretexting is another example. Pretexting is where the attacker provides some sort of scenario, invented scenario that makes it more likely that the victim is gonna provide information that they shouldn't. So they'll call you up and pretend to be your bank, or they'll call you up and pretend to be your help desk and say things like, we've seen unusual activity with your account, we're trying to stop it here before it impacts the entire organization, So you better give us your user ID and password. In other words, they've created some sort of pretext or scenario that makes it more likely for you to provide that information that you shouldn't. That's called pretexting. The next one is called baiting. Baiting is where attackers may put USB sticks in the front lobby of your building. And on that USB device or that USB stick, there could be some hidden executable files that provide back doors to the attackers to be able to get into your network. And so when you come into work and you see a USB stick on the floor, you're probably going to pick it up and plug it into your workstation or your laptop that's connected to the network and as soon as you do it executes that hidden executable that provides a back door for attackers to be able to get in. Again the best way to protect against these is to have really good policies but also make people educated and aware and have the proper skills so that they don't fall for these types of social engineering attacks that you're seeing here. Tailgating also referred to as piggybacking is something that relates to physical security only and this is where someone authenticates through a door and then someone follows through without having to authenticate because that door is still open. That's what is referred to as piggybacking or tailgating. And the one way that we protect against this, and in fact this is what you want to remember for purposes of the exam, is that we implement what are called man traps. And yes, they're still called man traps for whatever reason. But a man trap is a double door facility that only allows one person to go through at any given point in time. So that would prevent this problem of tailgating and piggybacking. And we call those man traps. Double door facilities that can prevent piggybacking and tailgating.
